title: _sync_l3 wipes local-only L3 columns (embedding, decay_rate, pinned, mode, ...) every 300s sync — dense retrieval, decay-forgetting, and pinning have never actually persisted
source: memory retrieval/audit deep-dive requested by Ash 2026-07-19 ('somethings feel off, like outdated facts... look into it')
current_state: MemoryTools._sync_l3() pulls l3_active_memory from Supabase every _sync_ttl_seconds (300s) by running `DELETE FROM l3_cache` followed by `INSERT INTO l3_cache (id, content, importance, category, active_until, created_at, invalid_at)` — only 7 columns. l3_cache actually has 23 columns; the other 16 (embedding, decay_rate, pinned, last_accessed_at, mode, conversation_id, scope, source_l2_id, kind, source_id, recompute_after, formula, superseded_by, surprise_score, goal_alignment, affect_tag) exist ONLY in SQLite (Supabase's l3_active_memory schema doesn't carry them) and are silently reset to their column defaults (NULL / 0) on every sync. Confirmed via grep + read: memory_write() populates these columns at write time (tools_memory.py:1152-1173), but the very next scheduled sync erases them. This explains three previously-unverified 'pillars' failing quietly in production: dense/hybrid retrieval (embedding always NULL post-sync -> retrieve() degrades to BM25-only), decay-forgetting (decay_rate/pinned/last_accessed_at reset -> effective_importance always computed from defaults), and pinning (pinned always reset to 0). Separately, kind='derived' rows (agent/caretaker.py backfill/recompute, T-125a) are NEVER written to Supabase at all — they are SQLite-only by design — so the unconditional `DELETE FROM l3_cache` destroys every derived row on every sync cycle regardless of column loss.
target_state: _sync_l3 preserves every local-only column for rows that survive the sync, and never touches kind='derived' rows (which have no Supabase counterpart to reconcile against). Only the 7 Supabase-owned columns are ever overwritten from the remote fetch; rows genuinely removed from l3_active_memory (hard-deleted remotely, e.g. by prune_l3_expired) are still removed locally, matching prior deletion semantics minus the collateral damage.
migration_plan: 1. Reproducing test: seed l3_cache with a row carrying embedding/decay_rate/pinned/mode/conversation_id set, call _sync_l3() with a fake Supabase response for that same id (7 synced columns only), assert the local-only columns survive post-sync. Second case: seed a kind='derived' row, call _sync_l3() with an empty/unrelated Supabase response, assert the derived row still exists.

2. Rewrite _sync_l3(): replace `DELETE FROM l3_cache` + 7-col INSERT with `INSERT INTO l3_cache (...7 cols...) VALUES (...) ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET <7 cols> = excluded.<7 cols>` per fetched row.
3. After upserting, delete local rows NOT present in the fetched id set AND NOT kind='derived' (mirrors old deletion semantics for remotely-removed rows without destroying derived-only rows).
4. Run existing test_l3_sync_ordering.py unmodified — must stay green (byte-level contract on the ordering/cap behavior).
risk_notes: Read-modify path only; no schema change. SQLite's ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE requires the id column to have a UNIQUE/PRIMARY KEY constraint, which l3_cache already has (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY). Demo-breaking if unfixed: the hybrid-retrieval and forgetting-ledger demo beats silently degrade to BM25-only / never-decays behavior in any long-running session that crosses one 300s sync boundary.
